volumeTitle: The Little Carthaginian. Pseudolus. The Rope
label: The Rope (Section 3.2.639 - Section 3.4.719b)

sections.label: Section 3.2.639
sections.original: Qui lubet maledicere ? equidem tibi bona optavi omnia .
sections.translation: Why do you choose to speak me ill ? For my part , I wished you everything that ’ s good .

sections.label: Section 3.2.640
sections.original: Bene equidem tibi dico , qui te digna ut eveniant precor .
sections.translation: And for my part , I ’ m speaking you well , in praying that things which you deserve may befall you .

sections.label: Section 3.2.641
sections.original: Obsecro , hoc praevortere ergo .
sections.translation: Prithee , do prevent this .

sections.label: Section 3.2.641b
sections.original: Quid negotist ?
sections.translation: What ’ s the matter , then ?

sections.label: Section 3.2.641c
sections.original: Mulieres
sections.translation: Two innocent women are inside here , in need of your aid , on whom , against law and justice , an injury has been , is being , glaringly committed here in the Temple of Venus . Besides , the Priestess of Venus

sections.label: Section 3.2.645
sections.original: indigne adflictatur .
sections.translation: is being disgracefully insulted .

sections.label: Section 3.2.645b
sections.original: Quis homo est tanta confidentia ,
sections.translation: What person is there of effrontery so great as to dare to injure the Priestess ? But these women , who are they ? Or what injury is being done to them ?

sections.label: Section 3.2.647b
sections.original: Si das operam , eloquar .
sections.translation: If you give me your attention , I ’ ll tell you . They have clung to the statue of Venus ; a most audacious fellow is now trying to tear them away . They ought , by rights , both of them to be free .

sections.label: Section 3.2.650
sections.original: Quis istic est qui deos tam parvi pendit ? paucis expedi .
sections.translation: What fellow is it that so lightly holds the Gods ? In a few words tell me .

sections.label: Section 3.2.651
sections.original: Fraudis sceleris parricidi periuri plenis simus ,
sections.translation: One most full of fraud , villany , parricide , and perjury ; a lawbreaker , an immodest , unclean , most shameless fellow ; to sum up all in one word , he is a Procurer ; why need I say more about him ?

sections.label: Section 3.2.654
sections.original: Edepol infortunio hominem praedicas donabilem .
sections.translation: Troth now , you tell of a man that ought to be handed over to retribution .

sections.label: Section 3.2.655
sections.original: Qui sacerdoti scelestus faucis interpresserit .
sections.translation: A villain , to seize the Priestess by the throat .

sections.label: Section 3.2.656
sections.original: At malo cum magno suo fecít hercle . ite istinc foras ,
sections.translation: By my troth , but he has done it at his own great peril . ( Calls aloud at his door .) Come you out of doors here , Turbalio and Sparax ; where are you ?

sections.label: Section 3.2.657b
sections.original: I obsecro intro , subveni
sections.translation: Prithee , do go in , and hasten to their rescue .

sections.label: Section 3.2.658b
sections.original: Iterum haud imperabo . sequimini hac .
sections.translation: And am I to call for them once more ?

sections.label: Section 3.2.658c
sections.original: Age nunciam ,
sections.translation: Come on now this instant , bid them tear his eyes out , just in the way that cooks do cuttle-fish [*] .

sections.label: Section 3.2.660
sections.original: Proripite hominem pedibus huc itidem quasi occisam suem .—
sections.translation: Drag the fellow out here by his legs , just like a slaughtered pig .

sections.label: Section 3.2.661
sections.original: Audio tumultum . opinor , leno pugnis pectitur .
sections.translation: I hear a scuffling ; the Procurer , I guess , is being belaboured with their fists ; I ’ d very much like them to knock the teeth out of the jaws of the most villanous fellow . But see , here are the women themselves coming out of the Temple in consternation .

sections.label: Section 3.3.664
sections.original: Núnc id est cum ómnium cópiarum átque opum ,
sections.translation: Now is that time arrived when destitution of all resources and aid ,

sections.label: Section 3.3.665
sections.original: aúxili , praésidi víduitas nós tenet .
sections.translation: succour and defence , overtakes us . Neither hope nor means is there to bring us aid , nor know we in what direction we should commence to proceed . In exceeding terror now are we both , in this our wretchedness . Such cruelty and such outrage

sections.label: Section 3.3.670
sections.original: fácta in nos ést modo hic íntus ab nóstro ero ,
sections.translation: have been committed towards us just now in-doors here by our master , who , in his villany , pushed down the old lady , the Priestess , headlong , and struck her in a very disgraceful manner , and with his violence tore us away from the inner side [*] of the statue . But as our lot and fortunes are now showing themselves ,

sections.label: Section 3.3.675
sections.original: pár moriri ést . neque est mélius morte ín malis ,
sections.translation: ’ twere best to die , nor in our miseries is there anything better than death .

sections.label: Section 3.3.676b
sections.original: Quid est ? quae íllaec orátiost ?
sections.translation: What ’ s this ? Whose words are those ? Why do I delay to console them ? ( Aloud .) Harkye , Palaestra ,

sections.label: Section 3.3.678
sections.original: Ámpelisca .
sections.translation: Ampelisca , harkye !

sections.label: Section 3.3.678b
sections.original: Óbsecro , quis est qui vocat ?
sections.translation: Prithee , who is it that calls us ?

sections.label: Section 3.3.679
sections.original: Quís is est qui nóminat ?
sections.translation: Who is it that calls me by name ?

sections.label: Section 3.3.679b
sections.original: Sí respexís , scies .
sections.translation: If you turn round and look , you ’ ll know .

sections.label: Section 3.3.680
sections.original: Ó salutís meae spés .
sections.translation: O hope of my safety !

sections.label: Section 3.3.680b
sections.original: Tace ac bóno animo es .
sections.translation: Be silent and of good courage ; trust me [*] .

sections.label: Section 3.3.680ab
sections.original: Sí modo id líceat , vis ne ópprimat ,
sections.translation: If only it can be so , let not violence overwhelm us .

sections.label: Section 3.3.681
sections.original: quaé vis vim mi áfferam ipsa ádigit .
sections.translation: That same which is driving me to commit violence on myself .

sections.label: Section 3.3.681b
sections.original: Ah , désine , nímis inepta es .
sections.translation: Oh , do leave off ; you are very silly .

sections.label: Section 3.3.682
sections.original: Desíste dictis núnciam miserám me consolári ;
sections.translation: Then do you leave off at once your consoling me in my misery with words .

sections.label: Section 3.3.683
sections.original: nisi quid re praesidium apparas , Trachalio , acta haec res est .
sections.translation: Unless you afford us protection in reality [*] , Trachalio , it ’ s all over with us .

sections.label: Section 3.3.684
sections.original: Certumst moriri quam hunc pati saevire lenonem in me .
sections.translation: I ’ m resolved to die sooner than suffer this Procurer to get me in his power . But still I am of woman ’ s heart ; when , in my misery , death comes into my mind , fear takes possession of my limbs . [*]

sections.label: Section 3.3.686
sections.original: mihi mortis , metus membra occupat . edepol ⋯ hunc acerbum .
sections.translation: By my troth , although this is a bitter affliction ,

sections.label: Section 3.3.687
sections.original: Bonum animum habete .
sections.translation: do have a good heart .

sections.label: Section 3.3.687b
sections.original: Nam , obsecro , unde áni mus mi invenitur ?
sections.translation: Why where , pray , is a good heart to be found for me ?

sections.label: Section 3.3.688
sections.original: Ne , inquam , timete ; adsidite hic in ara .
sections.translation: Don ’ t you fear , I tell you ; sit you down here by the altar .

sections.label: Section 3.3.688b
sections.original: Quid istaec ara
sections.translation: What can this altar possibly avail us more than the statue here within the Temple

sections.label: Section 3.3.690
sections.original: Veneris , quod amplexae modo , unde abreptae per vim miserae ?
sections.translation: of Venus , from which just now , embracing it , in our wretchedness , we were torn by force ?

sections.label: Section 3.3.691
sections.original: Sedete hic modo , ego hinc vós tamen tutabor . aram habete hanc
sections.translation: Only you be seated here ; then I ’ ll protect you in this spot . This altar you possess as though your bulwarks [*] ; these your fortifications ; from this spot will I defend you . With the aid of Venus , I ’ ll march against the wickedness of the Procurer .

sections.label: Section 3.3.694
sections.original: Tibi auscultamus et , Venús alma , ambae te obsecramus ,
sections.translation: We follow your instructions ( they advance to the altar and kneel ) ; and genial Venus , we both of us , in tears , implore thee ,

sections.label: Section 3.3.695
sections.original: aram amplexantes hanc tuam lacrumantes , genibus nixae ,
sections.translation: embracing this thy altar , bending upon our knees , that thou wilt receive us into thy guardianship , and be our protector ; that thou wilt punish those wretches who have set at nought thy Temple , and that thou wilt suffer us to occupy this thy altar with thy permission , we who last night were by the might of Neptune cast away ;

sections.label: Section 3.3.700
sections.original: ne invisas habeas neve idcirco nobis vitio vortas ,
sections.translation: hold us not in scorn , and do not for that reason impute it to us as a fault , if there is anything that thou shouldst think is not so well attended to [*] by us as it ought to have been .

sections.label: Section 3.3.702
sections.original: Venus , aequom has petere intellego : decet abs te id impetrari ;
sections.translation: I think they ask what ’ s just ; it ought , Venus , by thee to be granted . Thou oughtst to pardon them ; ’ tis terror forces them to do this . They say that thou wast born from a shell [*] ; take thou care that thou dost not despise the shells of these .

sections.label: Section 3.3.705
sections.original: sed optume eccum exit senex , patronus mihique et vobis .
sections.translation: But see , most opportunely the old gentleman is coming out , both my protector and your own .

sections.label: Section 3.4.706
sections.original: Éxi e fano , natum quantum est hominum sacrilegissime .
sections.translation: Come out of the Temple , you most sacrilegious of men , as many as have ever been born . Do you go ( calling to the WOMEN ) and sit by the altar . ( Not seeing them near the door .) But where are they ?

sections.label: Section 3.4.707b
sections.original: Huc respice .
sections.translation: Look round here .

sections.label: Section 3.4.708
sections.original: Optume , istuc volueramus . iube modo accedat prope .
sections.translation: Very good ; I wanted that [*] . Now bid him come this way . ( To LABRAX .) Are you attempting here among us to commit a violation of the laws against the Deities ?

sections.label: Section 3.4.710
sections.original: pugnum in os impinge .
sections.translation: ( To the SERVANTS , who obey with alacrity .) Punch his face with your fists .

sections.label: Section 3.4.710b
sections.original: Iniqua haec patior cum pretio tuo .
sections.translation: I ’ m suffering these indignities at your own cost .

sections.label: Section 3.4.711
sections.original: At etiam minitatur audax ?
sections.translation: Why , the insolent fellow ’ s threatening even .

sections.label: Section 3.4.711b
sections.original: Ius meum ereptum est mihi ,
sections.translation: I ’ ve been robbed of my rights ; you are robbing me of my female slaves against my will .

sections.label: Section 3.4.712b
sections.original: Cedo iudicem
sections.translation: Do you then find some wealthy man of the Senate of Cyrene as judge , whether these women ought to be yours , or whether they oughtn ’ t to be free ,

sections.label: Section 3.4.715
sections.original: nive in carcerem compingi te est aequom aetatemque ibi
sections.translation: or whether it isn ’ t right that you should be clapped into prison , and there spend your life , until you have worn the whole gaol out with your feet .

sections.label: Section 3.4.717
sections.original: Non hodie isti rei auspicavi , ut cum furcifero fabuler .
sections.translation: I wasn ’ t prepared to prophesy for this day that I should be talking with a hang-gallows [*] like yourself . ( Turning to DAEMONES .) You do I summon to judgment .

sections.label: Section 3.4.718b
sections.original: Cum istoc primum , qui te novit , disputa .
sections.translation: In the first place , try it with him who knows you .

sections.label: Section 3.4.719
sections.original: Tecum ago .
sections.translation: My suit is with yourself .

sections.label: Section 3.4.719b
sections.original: At qui mecum agendumst . suntne illae ancillae tuae ?
sections.translation: But it must be with myself . ( Pointing to the WOMEN .) Are these your female slaves ?
